narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your full-time study plans and a solid LSAT score of 38. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 59.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam—tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank around the middle of the pack, signaling to the model a potential struggle in sustaining strong academic performance. This outweighed your stronger Year 1 decile of 2, which had a milder push toward failure compared to averages. On a positive note, your LSAT score stood out above the dataset average, strongly nudging the model toward expecting bar passage success. Your family's upper-middle income also helped a bit, as it aligns with profiles that tend to fare better, much like most applicants. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, slightly below average, added a small influence toward the failure prediction, while your full-time enrollment choice provided a minor boost since that's the norm for high performers. Overall, compared to typical students who pass, your mid-tier undergraduate record and especially that low Year 3 decile set you apart in ways the model flagged as risks, even with your LSAT strength. We understand this is disappointing, especially given your dedication. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later academic deciles, perhaps supplementing with targeted bar prep experience to demonstrate resilience. You might also explore part-time options or additional test prep to highlight your potential. Keep pushing forward—many applicants turn rejections into success stories with focused improvements. We're rooting for you.
